title-group.article-title: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG Supernatant Improves GLP-1 Secretion Through Attenuating L Cell Lipotoxicity and Modulating Gut Microbiota in Obesity

contrib-group.aff: https://ror.org/02mh8wx89grid.265021.20000 0000 9792 1228Department of Gastroenterology and Hepatology, General Hospital, Tianjin Medical University, Tianjin, 300052 China

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Obesity is associated with decreased secretion of glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1), which may result from lipotoxic damage to L cells caused by elevated levels of free fatty acids (FFAs). Although the probiotic Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG) exhibits anti-apoptotic properties, its potential to protect L cells from lipotoxic damage remains uncertain. This study investigated the impact of LGG supernatant (LGGs) on NCI-H716 cells treated with palmitic acid (PA) to mimic lipotoxic injury, focusing on cell apoptosis and function. Transcriptome sequencing was used to explore the mechanism of the action of LGGs. Additionally, the effects of LGGs on body weight, glucose tolerance, GLP-1 secretion, and gut microbiota were assessed in a diet-induced obese mouse model. PA induced L cell apoptosis and decreased the level of prohormone convertase 1 (PC1) in a concentration- and time-dependent manner, leading to intracellular accumulation of proglucagon (GCG). LGGs significantly restored PA-induced downregulation of PC1, GCG accumulation, and cell apoptosis, mainly by inhibiting endoplasmic reticulum stress and downregulating the ATF3/Chop pathway. Overexpression of Chop or ATF3 partially reversed the protective effect of LGGs. Additionally, in the mouse model, LGGs improved obesity, insulin resistance, and glucose tolerance, and restored GLP-1 secretion, which may be related to LGGs’ inhibition of the ATF3/Chop pathway in L cells, regulation of gut microbiota composition, and enhancement of short-chain fatty acid production. Overall, LGGs can ameliorate high-fat diet-induced impairment of GLP-1 secretion by inhibiting lipotoxicity-mediated damage through the ATF3/Chop pathway and modulating the gut microbiota.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s12602-025-10564-9.

institution-wrap.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of China
National Key Research and Development Program of China

custom-meta.meta-value: © Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Obesity has emerged as a significant global public health issue [1]. Obesity leads to the occurrence of metabolic diseases such as cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes (T2DM), various cancers, and liver disease, seriously impacting individual quality of life and life expectancy [2–5]. Although dietary and lifestyle adjustments are currently the recognized initial treatment methods for obesity, they often fail to achieve satisfactory weight loss results [6]. Therefore, new treatment methods are urgently needed to achieve safe and effective weight loss. It is noteworthy that obese patients generally have impaired glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) secretion [7, 8]. GLP-1, a key intestinal peptide, regulates glucose homeostasis by promoting insulin release while suppressing glucagon secretion [9]. Additionally, this hormone influences energy balance through its effects on appetite reduction and gastric motility [10]. Although GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) agonists are currently used clinically for T2DM and obese patients and have achieved certain therapeutic effects, their use is limited due to potential gastrointestinal adverse reactions and possible increased risk of pancreatitis and even thyroid cancer [11–13]. Therefore, exploring safe and effective methods to improve endogenous GLP-1 secretion levels in obese patients is of great theoretical significance and application prospects for improving obesity [14].

Obese patients often have increased circulating levels of free fatty acids (FFAs) [15]. Lipotoxicity refers to the cellular dysfunction and apoptosis caused by the accumulation of excess FFAs in cells, a process that contributes to multiple disorders, especially obesity and T2DM [16–18]. L cells, as intestinal endocrine cells mainly distributed in the distal ileum and colon, are responsible for secreting GLP-1 and are crucial for maintaining blood glucose homeostasis [19]. The effects of lipotoxicity on L cell function and survival have gradually attracted researchers’ attention, because L cells, like β cells, are susceptible to glucolipotoxicity, which may lead to abnormal GLP-1 secretion in T2DM and obese patients [20]. In experimental models, researchers have employed palmitate (PA), the predominant saturated fatty acid found in blood, to treat GLUTag cells, creating an in vitro system that mimics hyperlipidemic conditions and their impact on intestinal L cells. The research found that PA led to DNA fragmentation, increased caspase-3 activity, and decreased viability of cells [21–23]. PA not only induces L cell apoptosis, but also impairs insulin signaling in cells [24–26]. The functional capacity of L cells and overall intestinal GLP-1 production depend critically on L cell survival. Therapeutic strategies aimed at enhancing L cell viability and preserving their population could represent a novel approach for addressing obesity-related disorders [27].

Probiotics, representing safe biological agents derived from natural sources, have garnered growing scientific interest due to their capacity to modulate human gut microbiota and influence lipid metabolism [28]. More and more studies have shown that supplementation with probiotics can effectively improve obesity. Among them, Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG) is a widely studied probiotic, and its role in human intestinal health has received increasing attention [29, 30]. Studies have shown that LGG has a strong anti-apoptotic effect. The p40 and p75 proteins extracted from the LGG supernatant (LGGs) inhibit apoptosis of epithelial cells and promote the growth of human and mouse colonic epithelial cells [31]. LGG can also prevent oxidative liver injury by activating Nrf2 [32]. Few studies have explored the potential protective effects of LGG against lipotoxicity-induced L cell death and impairment. The objective of this study is to investigate the effects of LGG on lipotoxicity-induced apoptosis and dysfunction of intestinal L cells, as well as to elucidate the underlying mechanisms.

sec.title: Culture of LGG
sec.p: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (ATCC53103, USA) was diluted in MRS broth (Solaibao, China) and cultured on MRS agar plates (Solaibao, China) at 37 °C for 24 h. The LGG strain was confirmed by 16S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) sequencing. LGG was cultured overnight at 37 °C to the logarithmic growth phase, then the culture medium was centrifuged to obtain LGGs. The number of LGG bacteria was determined by the plate count dilution method, and the concentration of LGG was 109 CFU/mL. LGGs were treated according to our previously reported method [29].

sec.title: Cell Culture
sec.p: The NCI-H716 cells (Wuhan Pu-nuo-sai Life Technology Co. Ltd) were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin at 37 °C in a cell culture incubator with 5% CO2. The cells were passaged every 2–3 days at a 1:3 ratio. The cell line was authenticated using STR analysis to confirm its identity. As NCI-H716 is a suspension cell line, it required pre-treatment with a Matrigel-coated cell culture plate for 24–48 h to allow adhesion and differentiation into mature endocrine cells before experimentation. PA (MCE, HY-N0830) solution was prepared as described in previous literature [25].

sec.title: CCK8
sec.p: Cells in the exponential growth phase were seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h before the original culture medium was removed. Six parallel replicates were set up for each experimental group. We followed the manufacturer’s instructions for the CCK-8 assay kit (Glpbio, USA) to perform the assay. This involved adding the CCK8 reagent to the cells, incubating them for 30 min, and then measuring the absorbance of the resulting solution at a certain wavelength (450 nm) using a microplate reader. The absorbance values obtained were then used to calculate cell viability.

sec.title: Quantitative Real-Time PCR Analysis
sec.p: Total RNA was extracted using TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen, USA). The RNA was reverse transcribed into first-strand cDNA following the manufacturer’s protocol for the TIANScript RT Kit (TIANGEN, China). Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) was conducted using the cDNA templates, gene-specific primers designed and synthesized by GENEWIZ (China), and ChamQ Universal SYBR qPCR Master Mix (Novizan, Nanjing, China). qPCR was performed under these conditions: stage 1, 95 °C for 30 s; stage 2, 40 cycles of 95 °C for 10 s and 60 °C for 30 s; stage 3, melting curve acquisition with 95 °C for 15 s, 60 °C for 60 s, and 95 °C for 15 s. Each qRT-PCR reaction was performed in triplicate to minimize the impact of random errors such as pipetting inaccuracies or differences in PCR cycling efficiency. β-Actin was used as the internal reference gene. Gene expression levels were analyzed using the 2-ΔΔCt method. All primer sequences are provided in Supplementary Table 1.

sec.title: Western Blot Analysis
sec.p: The proteins were extracted with RIPA lysate. After determining the protein concentrations of the samples by BCA assay (Solarbio, China), one-third volume of 4 × loading buffer (Solarbio, China) was added, and the samples were boiled at 100 °C for 10 min to fully denature the proteins. Each Western blot experiment was repeated at least three times to ensure the consistency of the protein expression patterns observed. Equivalent amounts of protein samples were separated by SDS-PAGE and transferred onto PVDF membranes (Millipore, USA). The membranes were blocked with 5% non-fat milk for 1 h. The membranes were then incubated with primary antibodies overnight at 4 °C. After incubation with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies, the protein bands were detected using an ECL. Image analysis was performed using ImageJ software (version 1.54). Main antibodies: β-actin (ABclonal, 1:100,000, AC026, China), Tubulin (ABclonal, 1:2000, AC015, China), Vinculin (ABclonal, 1:1000, A14193, China), PC1 (Abcam, 1:1000, ab220363, USA), C-caspase3 (Affinity, 1:1000, AF7022, USA), BIP (CST, 1:1000, 3177, USA), PERK (CST, 1:1000, 3192, USA), EIF2α (CST, 1:1000, 9722, USA), p-EIF2α (CST, 1:1000, 3398, USA), ATF3 (Affinity, 1:1000, DF3110, USA), ATF4 (Affinity, 1:1000, DF6008, USA), ATF6 (CST, 1:1000, 65,880, USA), Chop (Affinity, 1:1000, AF6277, USA).

sec.title: Flow Cytometry
sec.p: Flow cytometry and the Annexin V/PI Apoptosis Detection Kit (Solarbio, China) were used to detect cell apoptosis. Cells were divided into different groups. Each experiment was repeated three times as biological replicates to ensure the reliability and reproducibility. Cells from each group were collected by centrifugation (1000 rpm). Subsequently, the cells were resuspended in approximately 1 mL of pre-cooled PBS at 4 °C and centrifuged again to pellet the cells. The supernatant was carefully removed. Apoptosis detection was performed by flow cytometry, and data were analyzed using FlowJo software to calculate the percentage of apoptotic cells.

sec.title: Transcriptome Sequencing and Bioinformatics Analysis
sec.p: Transcriptome sequencing was conducted by Hangzhou Lianchuan Biological Information Technology Co., Ltd using 150-bp paired-end sequencing technology on the Illumina platform for RNA extraction, library preparation, and sequencing. This study utilized the online software Lianchuan Biological Cloud Platform (https://cloud.omicstudio.cn/tool.) for GO and KEGG enrichment analyses. Additionally, Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was conducted on the differentially expressed genes to identify enriched pathways and functions. Protein–protein interaction (PPI) network analysis was performed using the STRING database (https://cn.string-db.org). The output from the STRING database was further processed and visualized using Cytoscape tools.

sec.title: Plasmid Transfection
sec.p: The DDIT3 overexpression plasmid (pSLenti-EF1-EGFP-P2 A-Puro-CMV-DDIT3-3xFLAG-WPRE) and ATF3 overexpression plasmid (pcDNA3.1(+)-ATF3-3xFLAG-P2 A-EGFP), as well as the control plasmids, were purchased from OBiO Technology (OBiO, Shanghai, China). Lipofectamine 2000 transfection reagent was used to transfect the overexpression and empty plasmids into cells at 70–80% confluence. Following a 6-h incubation period post-transfection, the medium was refreshed with complete medium, and the incubation was prolonged. Total cellular RNA was isolated 24 h after the transfection, and the mRNA expression of the target genes was detected. Total cellular protein was harvested 48 h post-transfection, and the levels of the target proteins were detected.

sec.title: Animal Experiments
sec.p: Hua Fukang Experimental Animal Center (Beijing, China) provided C57BL/6 J mice (6 weeks) housed in specific pathogen-free rooms at 22 °C, 50–60% humidity, and 12 h of light/dark. Mice were randomly divided into four groups: standard diet (SD) + MRS medium (SD group); SD + LGGs (SL group); high-fat diet (HFD) + MRS medium (HF group); and HFD + LGGs group (HL group). Each group consisted of six mice. HF and HL groups were fed HFD (HFK Bioscience, Beijing, China) for 12 weeks to establish a diet-induced obesity (DIO) mouse model, while SD and SL groups were fed SD.

sec.title: Blood Glucose, GLP-1 and Insulin Measurements in Mice
sec.p: Before conducting the oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT), the mice were subjected to an overnight fast. A glucose solution (2 g/kg) was orally gavaged to each mouse. Blood samples were obtained from the tail vein. Glucose concentrations were assessed using a glucometer (Roche, China). ELISA kits (SenBeiJia Biological Technology, Nanjing, China) were utilized to quantify the levels of GLP-1 and insulin.

sec.title: Biochemical Analysis
sec.p: Liver function and lipidemic tests were detected with an automatic biochemical analyzer (BC240vet, Mindray, China).

sec.title: HE Staining
sec.p: Tissue samples were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde (Solaibao, China) for 24 h. Paraffin blocks were made and cut into 3–5-μm-thick sections. Sections were deparaffinized and rehydrated. Cell nuclei were stained with hematoxylin. Cytoplasm was counterstained with eosin. After dehydration and mounting, tissue morphology was examined under a light microscope (Leica, Germany).

sec.title: Oil Red O Staining
sec.p: Frozen liver sections were fixed in paraformaldehyde (Solaibao, China), stained with Oil Red O, de-stained with ethanol, counterstained with hematoxylin, dehydrated in ethanol, cleared in xylene, and mounted in gum. Lipid accumulation was examined under a microscope (Leica, Germany).

sec.title: Immunofluorescent Staining
sec.p: GCG was used as a marker for intestinal L cells. Primary antibodies were applied to the tissue sections and incubated overnight at 4 °C. The samples were then washed in 1 × PBS and incubated with diluted secondary antibodies in a dark environment. DAPI was employed to stain the nuclei. Fluorescence microscopes were used to observe and capture images. Three independent experiments were conducted, with a minimum of three fields of view for each experiment. Main antibodies: GCG (Abcam, 1:1000, ab92517, USA), GPR43 (Novus, 1:200, NBP3-12,190, USA).

sec.title: 16S rDNA Amplicon Sequencing
sec.p: The OMEGA Soil DNA Kit (M5635-02) (Omega Bio-Tek, USA) was utilized to extract total DNA from each sample. Before undergoing 2 × 250 bp paired-end sequencing on the Illumina NovaSeq platform, the PCR products were purified, quantified, and combined in equal amounts. This sequencing process was carried out by Shanghai Huada Gene Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bioinformatic analysis of microbial data was performed using QIIME2 2019.4. High-quality ASV tables were obtained through quality control, denoising, and clustering. Gene Cloud tools (https://www.genescloud.cn) were used to analyze the data.

sec.title: Short-Chain Fatty Acid Quantification
sec.p: An appropriate amount of sample was taken and mixed with 3 mL of hydrochloric acid (1 mol/L), vibrated, and then sonicated in an ice water bath for 10 min. It was then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. After centrifugation, 1 mL of supernatant was taken and extracted with 1 mL of dichloromethane. It was centrifuged again at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was then taken, filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, and injected into a gas chromatograph (Agilent 7890 A) for analysis.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: All data were statistically analyzed using SPSS 22.0 software. Normally distributed measurement data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Comparisons between groups were performed using independent sample t-tests. Comparisons among multiple groups were conducted using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s post hoc test for pairwise comparisons. Non-normally distributed measurement data were analyzed using Kruskal–Wallis tests. P values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

sec.title: The Effect of PA on H716 Cells Exhibits Concentration and Time Dependence
sec.p:
Considering that lipotoxic damage to cells typically requires more than 24 h to be observed in in vitro experiments, we treated H716 cells with different concentrations of PA for 24 h and observed changes in the protein levels of PC1, GCG, and cleaved-caspase3 (C-caspase3). The results showed that lower concentrations of PA (100–300 μM) did not significantly affect the C-caspase3 protein level, while PA (400 μM) led to a significant increase in the C-caspase3 protein level. Concurrently, lower concentrations of PA (100–200 μM) did not cause a decrease in the PC1 protein level, whereas PA (300 μM) treatment for 24 h resulted in a significant decrease in the PC1 protein level and a significant increase in the GCG protein level (Fig. 1A). The results suggest that lower concentrations of PA have a weaker effect on the function and apoptosis of H716 cells, while high concentrations of PA cause evident impairment of H716 cell function and quality (Fig. 1A). Next, we selected PA (400 μM) to treat cells for different durations to observe whether the effect of PA on H716 cells exhibits time dependence. The CCK8 assay showed that PA (400 μM) treatment for 12 h significantly impaired cell viability, which was 81.5% of the control group (Fig. 1B). Treatment for 24 h reduced cell viability to 64.3%, while treatment for 48 h caused excessive cell death, with a viability rate of only 20% (Fig. 1B). Therefore, we chose 24 h as the longest treatment duration. The effects of PA (400 μM) for varying durations on the function and apoptosis of H716 cells were examined. PA (400 μM) stimulation significantly increased the GCG mRNA level at 6 h and 12 h but had no significant effect on the GCG mRNA level at 24 h (Fig. 1C). Simultaneously, PA (400 μM) caused a significant increase in the PCSK1 mRNA level at 6 h, 12 h, and 24 h (Fig. 1D). Western blot results showed that PA (400 μM) treatment for 6 h led to a significant increase in the C-caspase3 protein level but had no significant effect on the PC1 and GCG protein levels. PA (400 μM) treatment for 12 h resulted in a significant decrease in the PC1 protein level and a significant increase in the GCG protein level, while PA (400 μM) treatment for 24 h further decreased the PC1 protein level and further increased the GCG protein level (Fig. 1D). The above results suggest that the lipotoxic damage caused by PA to H716 cells exhibits concentration and time dependence, leading to a decrease in the PC1 protein level, impairment of the GCG processing, and GCG protein accumulation in H716 cells.Fig. 1
The impact of PA at varying concentrations and durations on the function and apoptosis of H716 cells. A The impact of PA at varying concentrations on the protein levels of PC1, GCG, and C-caspase3 in H716 cells. B The impact of PA (400 μM) at varying durations on the survival of H716 cells. C The impact of PA (400 μM) at varying durations on the mRNA levels of GCG and PCSK1 in H716 cells. D The impact of PA (400 μM) at varying durations on the protein levels of PC1, GCG, and C-caspase3 in H716 cells. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001

sec.title: LGGs Inhibit Lipotoxicity-Induced Apoptosis and Functional Impairment in H716 cells
sec.p:
LGG intervention for 24 h had no significant effect on the mRNA levels of GCG and PCSK1 in H716 cells (Fig. 2A), suggesting that LGG cannot directly stimulate the production of GLP-1. We directly intervened H716 cells with LGGs for 24 h. The results showed that LGGs had no significant effect on the protein levels of PC1 and GCG, but significantly inhibited the level of C-caspase3 (Fig. 2B). We intervened H716 cells for 24 h with PA (400 μM) in combination with MRS or LGGs. PA stimulation significantly decreased the protein level of PC1 while significantly increasing the protein levels of GCG and C-caspase3. LGG intervention significantly restored the protein levels of PC1, GCG, and C-caspase3 (Fig. 2C), suggesting that LGG intervention significantly improved lipotoxicity-induced apoptosis and functional impairment in H716 cells. We further detected cell apoptosis using flow cytometry. Compared with the control group, PA significantly increased the proportion of apoptotic cells (60.0% vs. 12.3%, P < 0.001), while LGG intervention significantly reduced the proportion of apoptotic cells (28.1% vs. 60.0%, P < 0.001) (Fig. 2D).Fig. 2
Effects of LGGs on apoptosis and functional impairment in H716 cells. A Effects of LGG intervention alone on the mRNA levels of GCG and PCSK1 in H716 cells. B Effects of LGG intervention alone on the protein levels of PC1, GCG, and C-caspase3. C Effects of PA in combination with LGGs on the protein levels of PC1, GCG, and C-caspase3 in H716 cells. D Flow cytometry analysis of the effects of LGGs on lipotoxicity-induced apoptosis in H716 cells. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001

sec.title: Transcriptome Sequencing Used to Explore the Mechanism of Action of LGGs
sec.p:
To explore the mechanism by which LGGs ameliorate H716 cell apoptosis and functional impairment, we performed transcriptome sequencing on the PA combined with MRS group (C) and the PA combined with LGG group (L). The two groups of samples were significantly different overall (Fig. 3A). Compared with the C group, 2866 genes were significantly changed in the L group, among which 1581 genes were upregulated and 1285 genes were downregulated. We used a volcano plot to intuitively display the overall distribution of the differentially expressed genes. According to the set screening conditions (|FC|> 1.5 and P < 0.05), we identified 531 differentially expressed genes for subsequent GO and KEGG enrichment analysis (Fig. 3B). The GO enrichment analysis showed that the upregulated genes covered multiple aspects such as extracellular structures and functions, extracellular vesicles, acute phase response, complement activation, and signal receptor binding, while the downregulated genes were significantly enriched in multiple aspects such as cellular response to metal ions, protein folding, metabolic processes, and apoptosis regulation. The KEGG enrichment analysis showed that the upregulated genes covered multiple biological processes such as activation and regulation of the immune system, regulation of metabolic pathways, balance of cell proliferation and apoptosis, and drug metabolism, while the downregulated genes covered cellular adaptation to environmental changes, regulation of signal transduction, MAPK signaling pathway, regulation of metabolic pathways, and apoptosis pathway regulation (Fig. 3C–F).Fig. 3
Transcriptome sequencing analysis reveals the mechanism of action of LGGs. A PCA plot. B Volcano plot. C GO enrichment analysis (upregulated genes). D KEGG enrichment analysis (upregulated genes). E GO enrichment analysis (downregulated genes). F KEGG enrichment analysis (downregulated genes)

sec.title: LGGs Inhibit Lipotoxicity-Induced Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress in H716 Cells
sec.p:
Compared with the C group, the L group was closely associated with biological processes such as “cellular response to unfolded proteins,” “cellular response to oxidative stress,” and “endoplasmic reticulum stress-mediated intrinsic apoptotic signaling pathway” in the gene expression profile (Fig. 4A). Most of the disease-related genes were significantly downregulated. The expression pattern changes of relevant differentially expressed genes were intuitively displayed through a heatmap. Relative to the control treatment group, the endoplasmic reticulum stress-related genes and apoptosis-related genes such as ATF3, ATF4, DDIT3, GADD45G, PPP1R15 A, etc. showed an overall downward trend in the LGGs treatment group (Fig. 4B). In addition, we used the STRING database to analyze the interactions between proteins encoded by the top 100 differentially expressed genes. These genes were mainly clustered in the endoplasmic reticulum stress, heat shock protein, and apoptosis-related signaling pathways. Among them, key cell apoptosis genes such as ATF3, DDIT3, DDIT4, PPP1R15 A, and JUN were interlinked (Fig. 4C). Therefore, LGGs may exert a protective effect on H716 cells by inhibiting endoplasmic reticulum stress-related pathways. We detected changes in the major indicators of endoplasmic reticulum stress pathways. As expected, PA significantly increased the expression levels of BIP, PERK, p-EIF-2α, ATF3, ATF4, and Chop, while LGG treatment significantly reduced the expression levels of BIP, ATF3, ATF4, ATF6, and Chop, but had no significant effect on the level of PERK and even increased the expression levels of p-IRE1-α and p-EIF-2α (Fig. 4D), suggesting that LGGs did not inhibit the initiation of the endoplasmic reticulum stress process, but rather inhibited the expression of a series of ATF transcription factors, thereby inhibiting the expression level of Chop.Fig. 4
The impact of LGGs on lipotoxicity-induced endoplasmic reticulum stress in H716 cells. A GSEA enrichment analysis. B Heatmap of differentially expressed genes. C PPI interaction network. D The effect of LGGs on the main indicators of endoplasmic reticulum stress signaling pathways. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001

sec.title: The Beneficial Effects of LGGs Depend on the ATF3-Chop Pathway
sec.p:
To verify the effect of Chop on H716 cells, we overexpressed the Chop gene in H716 cells. The results showed that Chop overexpression had no significant effect on the mRNA levels of PCSK1 and GCG (Fig. 5A), but Chop overexpression significantly reduced the PC1 protein level and increased the protein levels of GCG and C-caspase3 (Fig. 5B), suggesting that Chop overexpression led to a decrease in PC1 protein level at the protein level, thereby causing an impairment in GCG cleavage. To verify whether LGGs exerted a series of protective effects by inhibiting Chop expression, we treated cells with or without Chop overexpression with PA intervention combined with or without LGGs treatment. LGGs still improved the dysfunction and apoptosis of H716 cells caused by lipotoxicity in the control group, while after Chop overexpression, the protective effect of LGGs on H716 cells was almost completely lost (Fig. 5C). Additionally, considering that LGGs had the strongest inhibitory effect on ATF3 and that the PPI interaction network suggested ATF3 as a key gene, we investigated whether LGGs exerted its effects through regulating the ATF3-Chop pathway by treating cells with or without ATF3 overexpression with PA intervention combined with or without LGGs treatment. ATF3 overexpression significantly reduced the inhibitory effect of LGGs on Chop, and ATF3 overexpression also significantly inhibited the protective effect of LGGs (Fig. 5D). The above results suggest that LGGs mainly protect H716 cells from lipotoxic injury by inhibiting the ATF3-Chop pathway.Fig. 5
The impact of overexpression of ATF3-Chop pathway on the protective effects of LGG. A The effect of Chop overexpression on PCSK1 and GCG mRNA levels in H716 cells. B The effect of Chop overexpression on PC1, GCG, and C-caspase3 protein levels in H716 cells. C The impact of Chop overexpression on the protective effects of LGG. D The impact of ATF3 overexpression on the protective effects of LGG. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001

sec.title: LGGs May Inhibit ATF3 Expression by Suppressing the JNK Pathway
sec.p:
Considering that the KEGG enrichment analysis suggested that LGGs inhibited the MAPK pathway, and the PPI analysis indicated that the JUN gene is in a core position, we speculate that LGG may inhibit ATF3 expression by suppressing the JNK pathway because previous studies have found that the MAPK/JNK pathway can inhibit ATF3 expression [33]. The research results showed that LGG significantly inhibited the activation of the JNK pathway, suggesting that LGG may inhibit the expression of ATF3 by suppressing JNK pathway activation (Fig. 6).Fig. 6
The impact of PA and LGGs on the JNK signaling pathway in H716 cells. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001

sec.title: LGGs Ameliorate Impaired GLP-1 Secretion and Inhibit the ATF3/Chop Pathway in DIO Mice
sec.p:
The animal experiment design was illustrated (Fig. 7A). As expected, LGGs improved weight gain caused by HFD. Each group of mice has a different growth curve for body weight (Fig. 7B). LGGs significantly improved HFD-induced hepatic steatosis and reduced lipid droplet area according to HE and Oil Red O stainings (Supplementary Fig. 1 A). In addition, LGGs ameliorated HFD-induced liver injury (Supplementary Fig. 1B-C), decreased the expression of hepatic lipogenic genes, and increased the expression of a lipolytic gene (Supplementary Fig. 1D). LGGs also reduced blood lipid levels (Supplementary Fig. 1E). These results suggest that LGGs significantly improved lipid metabolism in DIO mice. Fasting plasma insulin levels were increased in DIO mice but decreased after LGG supplementation (Supplementary Fig. 1F). The OGTT results showed that obese mice exhibited exacerbated hyperglycemic responses to oral glucose, and LGGs improved the ability of glycometabolism in DIO mice (Supplementary Fig. 1G). LGGs improved insulin resistance in DIO mice (Fig. 7C). We detected serum GLP-1 concentrations at 0, 30, and 120 min after glucose gavage in mice. The HFD and LGGs had no impact on the baseline GLP-1 concentrations, but LGGs notably improved the impaired GLP-1 secretion triggered by glucose in DIO mice (Fig. 7D). Fluorescent staining showed that LGGs significantly restored the damaged number of L cells in the ileum in DIO mice (Fig. 7E). HFD significantly increased the protein levels of ATF3 and Chop and decreased the protein level of PC1. In contrast, supplementation with LGGs significantly restored the protein levels of ATF3, Chop, and PC1 in obese mice (Fig. 7F). The above results suggest that LGGs may help improve impaired GLP-1 secretion in DIO mice.Fig. 7
The effect of LGGs on body weight and GLP-1 secretion in DIO mice. A Schematic diagram showing the experimental design. B Growth curves of mice. C Insulin resistance index of mice. D Serum GLP-1 concentrations at 0, 30, and 120 min and AUC during OGTT. E Immunofluorescent staining for L cells. F The effect of LGGs on ATF3, Chop, and PC1 protein levels in the ileum of mice. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001

sec.title: LGGs Activates the GPR43 Receptors on L Cells by Modulating the Gut Microbiota
sec.p:
To investigate whether LGGs could influence GLP-1 secretion through the gut microbiota, we performed 16S rRNA gene sequencing to clarify the composition of the gut microbiota. The abundances of many SCFA-producing bacteria, such as Butyricimonas, Parabacteroides, Alistipes, and Ruminococcus, were significantly lower in the HF group than in the SD group. LGGs significantly increased the abundances of SCFA-producing bacteria, such as Bacteroides, Alistipes, and Ruminococcus (Fig. 8A, B). As expected, HFD led to a significant decrease in colonic SCFA content, while LGGs restored the content of propionate and butyrate (Fig. 8C). HFD significantly decreased the colonic GCG mRNA level, while LGG supplementation restored the GCG mRNA level (Fig. 8D). The result of immunofluorescence double-staining showed that HFD significantly reduced the number of L cells and GPR43 expression in the colon, while LGGs restored the co-expression of GPR43 and L cells (Fig. 8E–G).Fig. 8
The effect of LGGs on gut microbiota composition and SCFAs production. A LEfSe analysis between SD and HF groups. B LEfSe analysis between HF and HL groups. C The content of acetate, propionate, and butyrate in feces. D The expression level of the GCG gene in the colon of mice. E Representative immunofluorescence images of the mouse colon stained for GPR43 (green) and GLP-1 (red) and merged. F Relative intensity of GPR43 immunofluorescence in the colon. G The number of GLP-1-positive cells in the colon. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The viability of mature L cells influences both their quality and the total GLP-1 secretion into the intestine. Elevated circulating FFAs are linked to obesity and T2DM [15].

Lipotoxicity is the cellular dysfunction and apoptosis resulting from excessive intracellular FFAs, significantly contributing to diseases like diabetes [34, 35]. As intestinal endocrine cells responsible for secreting GLP-1, L cells are crucial for maintaining blood glucose homeostasis. The precise impact of saturated fatty acids on intestinal L cells is not well understood. This study evaluated the influence of PA on the functionality and apoptosis of these cells. The results indicated that PA’s impact on L cells was both concentration- and time-dependent. A 24-h PA treatment significantly reduced the protein level of PC1 in L cells, increased the protein level of the GLP-1 precursor GCG intracellularly, and induced cell apoptosis. These effects may be attributed to oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction caused by high concentrations of PA [36], which subsequently affected L cell viability. In contrast, low-concentration and short-term PA treatments had minimal effects on cells. These findings are generally consistent with previous reports on lipotoxic effects [37]. Notably, while a 24-h PA treatment had no significant effect on GCG mRNA levels, intracellular GCG protein levels were significantly increased. This accumulation likely occurred because PA-induced endoplasmic reticulum stress reduced the protein level of the GLP-1 processing enzyme, leading to the intracellular accumulation of GCG. A comparable effect was noted in GLUTag cells exposed to endoplasmic reticulum stress inducers [38]. This study suggests that the saturated fatty acid PA induces L cell damage in a manner dependent on concentration and time. Oxidative stress and protein dysfunction may underlie the link between saturated fatty acids and metabolic diseases, offering a novel perspective for investigation.

We evaluated the protective effect of LGGs on PA-induced L cell apoptosis and dysfunction. Our study demonstrated that LGGs have a protective effect on L cells, which is consistent with previous reports of similar beneficial effects of LGGs [39]. LGG not only modulates the intestinal microbiota and enhances the intestinal mucosal barrier but also exhibits significant anti-apoptotic effects. For example, p40 and p75 proteins extracted from LGG supernatant inhibit cytokine-induced epithelial cell apoptosis and promote the growth of human and mouse colonic epithelial cells [31]. Compared with existing studies, our investigation analyzed the molecular mechanisms of the anti-apoptotic effects of LGGs more systematically and comprehensively. The results demonstrated that LGGs effectively inhibit the expression of various endoplasmic reticulum stress-related genes induced by PA, especially the ATF transcription factors and their downstream target gene Chop. Previous studies have confirmed that Chop is an important transcription factor under endoplasmic reticulum stress conditions that can induce a series of apoptosis-related genes, leading to cell apoptosis [40, 41]. Our study further verified the key role of the ATF3/Chop pathway in the protective effect of LGGs through overexpression experiments. Moreover, we discovered that LGGs may inhibit the expression of ATF transcription factors by suppressing the JNK pathway. This mechanism is supported by the observation that LGGs do not inhibit the endoplasmic reticulum stress process from the initial pathway, and numerous studies have revealed the regulatory role of the JNK pathway on ATF transcription factors [42, 43]. By evaluating the protective effect of LGGs using a human L cell model, this study provides evidence for the potential clinical application of LGG.

There is a close association between intestinal dysbiosis and impaired GLP-1 secretion [44]. LGG is one of the most extensively studied probiotics. Numerous studies have demonstrated that LGG can improve insulin sensitivity by regulating the host intestinal microbial community structure and exert hypoglycemic and weight-reducing effects in obese mouse models [45, 46]. Although our study and previous investigations have indicated that LGG cannot directly stimulate GLP-1 synthesis and release in intestinal L cells [47], our research results demonstrated that LGGs significantly increased the number of GLP-1-positive cells in the intestinal tissues of obese mice, enhanced the abundance of SCFA-producing bacteria in the intestine, and elevated the levels of propionate and butyrate in the colonic contents. The increase in these SCFAs can activate receptors such as GPR43 in the colonic tissue, stimulating both transcriptional expression and protein secretion of GLP-1 in L cells. In summary, the SCFA-mediated signaling pathway may represent one of the molecular mechanisms through which LGGs regulate host metabolism by optimizing the intestinal microecology.

However, our study has several limitations. Our transcriptome analysis identified numerous differentially expressed genes, but their specific functions and interactions need further study. Furthermore, the protective mechanisms of LGG likely involve multiple signaling pathways and molecules, and future research should focus on elucidating the detailed mechanisms of action of these key molecules. The NCI-H716 cell line was used for in vitro experiments. Although it can simulate the function of L cells, as an immortalized cell line, its differentiation status and signaling pathway responses may differ from primary L cells. In addition, PA treatment, as a lipotoxicity model, only reflects the effects of saturated fatty acids (such as palmitic acid), while obese patients have an imbalance in the proportions of multiple FFAs in their bodies. Finally, the clinical application potential of LGG needs to be evaluated in a broader population to determine its safety and efficacy. Further research should investigate its in vivo mechanisms and assess its broader clinical utility.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p:
Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.ESM 1
(DOCX 1.75 MB)ESM 2
(XLSX 10.0 KB)

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We are grateful to Figdraw (www.figdraw.com) for technical support.

notes.title: Author Contribution

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 81900487, 82100565) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2019YFC0119505).

notes.title: Data Availability
notes.p: All data are available in the manuscript or upon request to the authors.

notes.title: Ethics Approval and Consent to Participate
notes.p: The study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Tianjin Medical University General Hospital (NO. IRB2022-DWFL-447).

notes.title: Conflict of Interest
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
